coredata.dc:title: Sulfanilic acid assisted-synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots with visible pH response for sensing nitrofuran antibiotics
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2025 Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier Ltd. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Guo, Ruizhen
Deng, Guoqing
Wu, Hui
Fan, Wenying
Zhang, Yi
coredata.dc:description: The abuse of antibiotics posed a serious threat to food security and human health, therefore it was of vital importance for fast and sensitive sensing of nitrofuran antibiotics (NFs). In this study, carbon dots materials with bright blue fluorescence were developed by applying an easy hydrothermal method from rapeseed meal assisted by sulfanilic acid. The sulfanilic acid could not only supply N and S elements, but also effectively break the internal robust chemical structure of rapeseed meal. The skillful doping of N and S contributed to a high QY of 32.4 % for 2SRM-CDs. The luminescent sites of 2SRM-CDs changed with pH, and therefore exhibited an interesting visual response to pH variation. Besides, the 2SRM-CDs showed satisfactory sensitivities and selectivities for furazolidone, nitrofurantoin and nitrofurazone with the limits of detection of 0.354 μM, 0.258 μM and 0.327 μM, when their concentrations were 1.0–330 μM, 0.5–260 μM and 0.5–230 μM,respectively. Furthermore, the as-developed sensor could be successfully used to detect NFs in actual samples, which demonstrated potential in fields such as environmental monitoring and food analysis.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Rapeseed meal
Nitrofuran
Fluorescence detection

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Determination of three NFs in actual samples
The synthesis of N, S co-doped 3SRM-CDs
Fluorescent properties of 2SRM-CDs with diverse pH values
Measurement of NFs in actual samples

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (A) Fluorescence emission spectra of carbon dots prepared with different amounts of sulfanilic acid (changing from 0 to 2 g), (B) The effect of hydrothermal reaction temperature (changing from 160 to 200 °C) on the fluorescence intensity and synthetic yield of 2SRD-CDs.
(A) The TEM image, (B) the size distribution and (C) the FT-IR spectrums of 2SRM-CDs.
(A) The full scan XPS spectra of 2SRM-CDs; the high-resolution spectra of (B) C 1s, (C) O 1s, (D) N 1s, (E) S 2p and (F) the nitrogen and sulfur contents.

(A) UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescent spectra of 2SRM-CDs (inset: 2SRM-CDs solution under natural light and UV light); (B) the emission spectra of 2SRM-CDs solution scanned at different excitation wavelengths.

(A) Changes of emission wavelength and luminescence color of 2SRM-CDs at different pH values; (B) Fluorescence intensity plot of 2SRM-CDs cycling between pH 5–12 at 370 nm excitation; (C) Color change of 2SRM-CDs solution at pH 3–12 under 370 nm UV light irradiation. (For interpretation of the references to color in this figure legend, the reader is referred to the Web version of this article.)

(A) Fluorescent spectra of 2SRM-CDs with different concentrations of FZD, inset shows the fluorescence of 2SRM-CDs without and with FZD under UV lamp. (B) A linear relationship between ln (F0/F) and FZD (1.0–330 μ M). (C) Fluorescence spectra of 2SRM-CDs at different concentrations of NFT, inset shows the fluorescence of 2SRM-CDs without and with NFT under UV lamp. (D) A linear relationship between ln (F0/F) and NFT (0.5–260 μ M). (E) Fluorescence spectra of 2SRM-CDs at different concentrations of NFZ, inset shows the fluorescence of 2SRM-CDs without and with NFZ under UV lamp. (F) A linear relationship between ln (F0/F) and NFZ (0.5–230 μ M).

(A) Selectivity of the 2SRM-CDs for FZD, NFZ, and NFT over some controls; (B) Some interference of the 2SRM-CDs for FZD, NFZ, and NFT (the concentrations of substances were 200 μM); (C) The photographs of 2SRM-CDs in the presence of controls under UV light.
Comparison of different analytical methods for NFs measurement.
The NFs measurement in actual samples.

head.ce:title: Sulfanilic acid assisted-synthesis of nitrogen and sulfur co-doped biomass carbon dots with visible pH response for sensing nitrofuran antibiotics

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Food Science and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Chemical Engineering
Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Hubei Longzhong Laboratory, Xiangyang, 441000, Hubei, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Hubei Longzhong Laboratory, Xiangyang 441000, Hubei. China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Food Science and Chemical Engineering, Hubei University of Arts and Science, Xiangyang, 441053, Hubei Province, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Food Science and Chemical Engineering
Hubei University of Arts and Science

ce:footnote.ce:note-para: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The abuse of antibiotics posed a serious threat to food security and human health, therefore it was of vital importance for fast and sensitive sensing of nitrofuran antibiotics (NFs). In this study, carbon dots materials with bright blue fluorescence were developed by applying an easy hydrothermal method from rapeseed meal assisted by sulfanilic acid. The sulfanilic acid could not only supply N and S elements, but also effectively break the internal robust chemical structure of rapeseed meal. The skillful doping of N and S contributed to a high QY of 32.4 % for 2SRM-CDs. The luminescent sites of 2SRM-CDs changed with pH, and therefore exhibited an interesting visual response to pH variation. Besides, the 2SRM-CDs showed satisfactory sensitivities and selectivities for furazolidone, nitrofurantoin and nitrofurazone with the limits of detection of 0.354 μM, 0.258 μM and 0.327 μM, when their concentrations were 1.0–330 μM, 0.5–260 μM and 0.5–230 μM,respectively. Furthermore, the as-developed sensor could be successfully used to detect NFs in actual samples, which demonstrated potential in fields such as environmental monitoring and food analysis.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nitrofuran (NFs) antibiotics are a general term for 5-nitro-2-substituted furan derivatives, including furazolidone (FZD), nitrofurantoin (NFT), and nitrofurazone (NFZ). NFs are a class of broad-spectrum antibiotics with certain bactericidal ability [1,2]. NFs have the advantages of wide antibacterial range, relatively low price, and excellent therapeutic effect, and have been widely used in the breeding process of edible animals [3–6]. Despite its therapeutic value, excessive use of NFs may lead to genetic mutagenesis and even pose a threat to human health by being ingested through the food chain [7]. Many regions and countries have explicitly banned the use of NFs drugs and introduced a series of regulations that prohibit the detection of NFs residues in edible animal food [8]. Therefore, it is urgent to take actions to build up sensitive technologies for detecting residual antibiotics in the environment. Until now, although instrument based measurement methods such as capillary electrophoresis [9], enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [10], high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [11], high-performance liquid chromatography-tandem mass spectrometry (HPLC-MS) [12], electrochemistry [13] and spectrophotometry [14] can achieve sensitive detection of antibiotics, these approaches involve costly equipment, time-consuming, and complicated operating steps [15]. In comparison, fluorescence based analysis methods are gradually gaining favor among researchers over the years considering their superiorities of high efficiency, convenience, and sensitivity. Various fluorescence materials, including metal organic frameworks [16], organic fluorescence polymers [17], copper nanoclusters [18] and carbon materials [19,20] have already been established as the fluorescent materials for sensing NFs.

Among them, carbon based quantum dot materials (CDs) have attracted more and more attentions considering their simplicity of preparation, low-toxic, excellent biocompatibility, excellent optical properties and high stability and tenability [21]. They have also shown potential in analyzing antibiotic substances. For instance, Li et al. successfully prepared red fluorescent carbon dots from neutral red and thiourea via one-pot hydrothermal treatment, they could serve as a fluorescence sensor to measure tetracycline antibiotics [22]. However, carbon dots materials face challenges including inadequate fluorescence efficiency and sensitivity, which evidently limit the extensive applications [23]. In order to improve the above-mentioned shortcomings, some manners have been developed, for example surface modification, morphology regulation as well as the most commonly used heteroatom doping [24]. It was reported that heteroatom (N, P, S, etc.) doping could effectively improve the element distribution, electronic configuration as well as photoluminescence characters of carbon materials [24,25]. Sun et al. fabricated N-doped graphene quantum dots as a rapid fluorescence sensor for FZD detection with a LOD of 0.29 μM and a measuring scope of 5–130 μM [26]. Huang et al. synthesized phosphorus, nitride-doped carbon nanosheets from melamine and phosphonitrilic chloride trimer for sensitively sensing NFs [24]. Manshadi et al. synthesized the N, S co-doped carbon dots from l-cysteine amino acid and ethylene glycol, and developed for the fluorescence detection of furazolidone in the scope of 8.0–250.0 μg/L and LOD of 2.1 μg/L [20]. From the perspectives of cost and environmental protection, it is more attractive to use biomass waste including soy-milk [27], Clematis Chinensis Osbeck [19], orange juice [28], watermelon peel [29], etc. instead of traditional chemicals as raw materials to prepare carbon dots. In this respect, Zhang et al. fabricated carbon dots from coix seed by an easy hydrothermal process, and used as a selective sensor to determine FZD with a LOD of 0.096 μM and a linear scope of 0.5–100 μM [7]. However, carbon dots typically prepared from biomass waste often have low quantum yield (QY) and poor fluorescence performance, and require heteroatoms doping to improve their performance [30]. Rapeseed cake is a kind of rich but cheap waste generated during the oil production process, which naturally rich in a large amount of nitrogen element, and hence is a potential N-rich biomass-based agricultural waste materials to prepare N-doped CDs materials [31]. In our recent work, we successfully synthesized nitrogen doped carbon dots emitting blue light using rapeseed cake as raw material for highly sensitive detection of baicalein [32]. However, the complex natural structure of rapeseed meal makes it difficult to depolymerize and re-polymerize to form the high quantum yield carbon dot materials.

Based on the above facts, in present study, we raised a facile “one-step” hydrothermal method to synthetize S, N co-doped carbon materials using rapeseed meal as the primary source of their raw material. To conquer the complicated molecular architecture of rapeseed meal and improve the QY value of the prepared carbon dots, sulfanilic acid was acted as the“top-down” scissor and N–S doping source. Nitrogen and sulfur contents were skillfully co-doped in the process of rapeseed meal cracking and carbon dots formation by an effective hydrothermal method (Scheme S1). The proposed preparation mechanism for N, S co-doped CDs was conferred. We also deeply studied the relation between the fabrication, structural characters and optical features. The bright fluorescence of prepared materials was obviously quenched after addition of NFs as a result of the effects of inner filter effect and static quenching. Moreover, the pH visible response, detecting sensibility and selectivity of CDs were also surveyed. The fabricated CDs materials can also be applied for detecting NFs in some real samples.

The rapeseed cake used in the experiments was obtained from an oil processing factory in Xiangyang. Sulfanilic acid, KCl, LiCl, Pb(NO3)2, CdCl2, MgCl2, K2CO3, K2HPO4, d-threonine (D-Thr), d-alanine (D-Ala), d-methionine (D-Met), d-serine (D-Ser), Chloramphenicol (CAP), Cefalexin (CFX), Pyrazinamide (PZA), Furazolidone (FZD), Nitrofurantoin (NFT), and nitrofurazone (NFZ) were purchased from Macklin LTD. (Shanghai, China), and all reagents were of analytical grade without additional purification.

A novel N and S co-doped biomass carbon dots assisted by sulphanilic acid was fabricated by an easy hydrothermal process. Briefly, the crushed rapeseed meal (1.0 g) and sulphanilic acid (0.5 g, 1.0 g or 2.0 g) were poured into 50 mL pure water under magnetic stirring for 5 min, the mixture was poured into a 100 mL autoclave. After 2 h of hydrothermal reaction maintaining at 200 °C for 6 h, the dark-brownish solution was collected by centrifugation. The obtained yellow solution was dialyzed by a dialysis bag (1000 Da), then the dialysate was freeze-dried to get brown xSRM-CDs (x = 1, 2, 3, refers to the dosage of sulphanilic acid of 0.5 g, 1.0 g and 2.0 g). For comparison, RM-CDs were also prepared using the same method, except that sulfanilic acid was not added.

The fluorescence tests were performed by an F-2700 fluorescence spectrophotometer (Hitachi, Japan), and the spectral slit widths were set as 5 nm. The fluorescent lifetime data was recorded on an FELIX32 Fluorescence Lifetime spectrometer (Photon Technology International, USA). The UV–vis spectra of CDs were obtained by a UV-2600 UV–vis spectrophotometer (Shimadzu, Japan). The Talos F200X (Thermo Fisher Scientific, United States) was applied to study transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images with the voltage of 200 kV for determining the structure and particle sizes of the materials. The infrared (FT-IR) infrared (FT-IR) spectra of CDs were recorded on an IR PRESTIGE-21 spectrometer within the scope of 500–4000 cm−1. The chemical properties of material surface were surveyed by a Thermo Scientific ESCALAB Xi + X-ray photoelectron spectrometer. The pH values of solutions were tested by a PHS-25 pH-meter (Shanghai, China).

The fluorescent quantum yield (QY) was tested when quinine sulfate as a standard reference (QY is 54 % for 0.1 M H2SO4) [33]. The QY value was decided in following equation:Where Q, A, I and η refer to the quantum yield, absorbance value, fluorescence intensity and the refractive index of solvent, respectively. The S and X denote as reference and sample.

Various amounts of NFs like FZD, NFT or NFZ were mixed with the carbon dots solution for reaction 60 s. Then, the fluorescent spectra of each mixing solution were recorded under 370 nm excitation. F0 and F denote the fluorescent intensity values of CDs solution and CDs solution with NFs, respectively. Linear fitting was performed according to the changes in fluorescence intensity for establishing the linear curves for determining NFs.

The 100 μM of prospective interferences, such as some metal ions, anions, amino acids and antibiotics was mixed with the 2SRM-CDs solution respectively. The fluorescent intensity of diverse solutions was determined under an excitation wavelength of 370 nm. Moreover, the influences of 2SRM-CDs with three NFs were surveyed by controlling whether to add the above-mentioned potential interferences or not.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Determination of three NFs in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: The feasibleness of prepared materials for NFs measurement in some actual samples including lake water and human urine samples was investigated, the diverse amounts (20, 40 and 60 μM) of three NFs were added into the lake water and human urine samples, and the recovery tests were calculated on the basis of the fitted linear formulas.

ce:section.ce:section-title: The synthesis of N, S co-doped 3SRM-CDs
ce:section.ce:para: A novel N, S co-doped biomass-derived CDs assisted by sulfanilic acid was prepared by a simple hydrothermal method (Scheme S1). It is well known that rapeseed meal generated during the oil production process, naturally rich in a large amount of nitrogen element, and hence is a potential N-rich biomass-based agricultural waste materials to prepare N-doped CDs materials [32]. However, its complex natural structure makes it difficult to depolymerize and re-polymerize to form the carbon dot materials. Given the fact that acids can effectively catalyze the depolymerization of biomass into small molecule substances including polyphenols, aromatic aldehydes, etc, sulfanilic acid can acted as the“top-down” scissor to conquer its complex structure and break the robust C–O and C–N bonds in the process of hydrothermal treatment [34,35]. The formed reactivity substances could polymerize with each other to produce the nano-sized carbon materials. At the same time, sulfanilic acid also serves as the N–S doping source, the two elements could be skillfully co-doped in CDs in the process of rapeseed meal cracking and carbon dots formation by an effective hydrothermal method [36].

The effect of key synthesis conditions including dosage of sulfanilic acid, preparation temperature on the fluorescent behavior of the SRM-CDs was surveyed by fixing the excitation wavelength of 370 nm. As presented in Fig. 1 (A), the RM-CDs synthesized solely from rapeseed cake have a significantly smaller emission wavelength with poor fluorescence intensity and low QY (14.8 %), and emit fluorescence in navy blue color. The SRM-CDs derived from rapeseed meal prepared with the assistance of sulfanilic acid have significantly larger emission wavelengths, and emit bright blue fluorescence. As the dosage of sulfanilic acid increased, the fluorescent intensity of SRM-CDs gradually increased, and 2SRM-CDs (mass ratio of rapeseed meal to sulfanilic acid 1:1) has a high fluorescence intensity, with its QY value increasing to 32.4 %. Continuing to increase the dosage of sulfanilic acid, there were no significant increases in the fluorescence intensity and QY for 3SRM-CDs. Thus the mass ratio of rapeseed meal to sulfanilic acid of 1:1 was regarded as the suitable amount of sulfanilic acid for next researches. In addition, the influences of preparation temperature on the fluorescence value and CDs yield were further studied within the scope of 180–220 °C, the results are displayed in Fig. 1(B). As the hydrothermal temperature enhanced from 180 to 200 °C, both the fluorescence intensity and CDs yield increased obviously. Further increase of the temperature, the fluorescence intensity and CDs yield gradually diminished. In addition to quantum yield, the synthesis yield of carbon dots is also crucial for the practical application of CDs. Commonly, a relatively high temperature around 200 °C is required for hydrothermal preparation of CDs, but a too high temperature possibly degrade the CDs and make them to aggregate into the larger particles. As a result, the optimal preparation temperature of 200 °C was adopted for further experiments.

The structural property of 2SRM-CDs was studied by TEM in Fig. 2 (A), it was seen that the 2SRM-CDs were the uniformly dispersed spherical particles, the morphology was consistent with the materials reported in the literature [37]. Fig. 2(B) shows the particle size distribution of the prepared 2SRM-CDs, it confirmed that particle size of 2SRM-CDs was relatively small, primary distributed between 3 and 5 nm. On the basis of the statistical analysis, the average grain size of 2SRM-CDs was 4.38 ± 0.35 nm. The results of various CDs FT-IR are presented in Fig. 2(C) to identify their surface functional groups. The broad peak centered at ca. 3445 cm−1 was identified for all materials, it was related to the presence of O–H and N–H groups. A non-salient band at 2935 cm−1 was ascribed to the vibration of C–H. Some absorption peaks located at 1625 cm−1 and around 1450-1640 cm−1 were corresponded to the vibrational of CO band and the aromatic CC, respectively. Besides, the absorption band that centered at 1128 cm−1 was connected with the ethers, suggesting that materials possessed abundant surface oxygen containing groups [38]. It is worth noting that some additional peaks at 1128-1180 cm−1 appeared for SRM-CDs materials, which was attributed to the presence of the stretching vibrations of –SO2 in sulfonic acids. Moreover, the peaks around at 600-900 cm−1 for SRM-CDs materials were also found, which were mainly due to the presence of C–S bond and S–O bond, confirming the expected introduction of S content in the form of sulfonic acid groups [35,39]. The results of FT-IR suggested that the presence of abundant hydrophilic functional groups such as phenolic –OH, –COOH, –CO-, -NH- and –SO3H groups scattered outside the SRM-CDs, which was conducive to increasing their QY, as well as the water-solubility of CDs for sensing [40].

The chemical composition information of materials was further thoroughly surveyed by XPS. According to the full scan spectra in Fig. 3 (A) , several obvious peaks around at 284.6 eV, 399.5 eV and 531.4 eV were found, which corresponded to the binding energy of C 1s, N 1s, and O 1s, respectively. This result revealed the presence of C, N, and O elements in RM-CDs [35,37]. However, SRM-CDs materials clearly showed an additional peak that centered at 167.7 eV of S 2p, further proving the successful doping of S element in SRM-CDs materials [40]. It was seen from C1s high-resolution spectra in Fig. 3 (B) that it was divided into four peaks, i.e. 284.5 eV, 285.2 eV, 285.9eV and 288.2 eV, that were attributed to C–C/CC, C–N/C–S, C–O and CO, respectively [41]. The O 1s high-resolution spectra of all materials in Fig. 3 (C) could be divided into two peaks centered at 531.5 eV and 532.8 eV, they were related to the presence of CO and O–C, respectively [19]. According to Fig. 3 (D), there were three fitting peaks at 398.7 eV, 400.1 eV and 401.1 eV in N 1s spectrum for all materials, which were ascribed to pyridine nitrogen C–N, graphitic nitrogen SP3 hybrid N-(C)3 and pyrrolic nitrogen N–H, respectively [21,42]. Moreover, double peaks located at 167.5 eV and 168.7 eV were identified for SRM-CDs materials based on the high-resolution spectrum of S 2p in Fig. 3 (E), which were attributed to SO and C-SOx respectively, suggesting the binding of –SO2 (and –SO3) groups on SRM-CDs [42]. In order to elucidate the possible effects of N and S doping on material properties, the N and S contents of the materials were quantified on the basis of XPS results, as shown in Fig. 3 (F). It was found that the use of sulfanilic acid in the carbon dot preparation process did not increase the nitrogen content of the material, but significantly increased the S content. Based on the above results, we can speculate that SRM-CDs had fluorescence characteristics different from RM-CDs, which were highly likely due to the doping of S element.

The optical characters of 2SRM-CDs were thoroughly surveyed by means of the UV–vis absorption spectra and fluorescence spectra. From Fig. 4 (A) of the UV–Vis absorption spectra of 2SRM-CDs, it showed an exciton adsorption band centered around 230 nm, it was related to the formation of aromatic sp2 π-π ∗ transition conjugated carbon backbone [20,43]. Results of fluorescence characteristics suggested that 2SRM-CDs had the best excitation (Ex) wavelength of 370 nm and emission (Em) wavelength of 455 nm. As shown from Fig. 4(A), the 2SRM-CDs solution presented a clear pale yellow under sunlight, but it emitted a bright blue fluorescence under an ultra violet light at 370 nm. From Fig. 4(B), 2SRM-CDs displayed a fluorescent emission performance that depended on the excitation wavelength within the investigated scope of 355–385 nm, and the fluorescent strength presented a clear trend of first increasing and then decreasing. Apparently, 370 nm was regarded as the best excitation wavelength, and 2SRM-CDs presented the strongest fluorescent signal. Moreover, the 2SRM-CDs with N–S co-doping showed an excitation-independent emission behavior, the reason was that the doped S contents seemed to degrade the O-states and improve the N-state, contributing to the various surface emissive states [32,44].

Fluorescence stability of nano-sensor is an important factor for guaranteeing the accuracy and reliability of the determination by CDs, and thus, the effects of diverse environmental conditions such as storage time and environmental temperature, ion strength, UV exposure on the fluorescent intensities of 2SRM-CDs were further evaluated. As shown in Fig. S1, the fluorescent intensities of 2SRM-CDs only reduced slightly at both 4 °C and 25 °C, indicating that the materials preserved a relatively good fluorescence stability within 10 days. Fig. S2 shows the variations of fluorescent intensity of 2SRM-CDs solutions with the diverse concentrations of NaCl. As the NaCl concentration increased to as high as 0.5 M, the fluorescence intensity of 2SRM-CDs only showed an insignificant decrease, suggesting that their fluorescence intensity had a good stability at high salt concentrations. Moreover, the 2SRM-CDs showed almost unchanged fluorescence intensity values by 370 nm UV irradiation for 5 min (Fig. S3), indicating their excellent photostability. Based on the above results, the synthesized nano-sensors in this study exhibited good stability in complex environmental conditions, which can guarantee the accurate and reliable detection results in sensing fields.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescent properties of 2SRM-CDs with diverse pH values
ce:section.ce:para: The pH responses of 2SRM-CDs were examined by investigating the fluorescent characteristics of 2SRM-CDs aqueous solutions at distinct pH levels. As presented in Fig. 5 (A), as the pH level of the 2SRM-CDs solution was within the range of 3–5, the fluorescent emission peak position of 2SRM-CDs under the excitation wavelength of 370 nm remained basically unchanged, and centered at around 460 nm. As the pH continued to increase to 6–12, the 2SRM-CDs exhibited a singular pH response, and the emission peak showed a clear blue shift to 445 nm at excitation wavelength of 370 nm. The cause might be that the 2SRM-CDs contained plentiful nitrogen, sulfur, and oxygenated groups, the –OH groups in the alkaline environment could binding with 2SRM-CDs surface via hydrogen bonding to form new luminescent sites, contributing to a change of the fluorescence color to navy blue [22,35]. Moreover, the fluorescence stability during repeated pH change was surveyed by adjusting the pH values within the scope of 5–11 with 10 cycles. From Fig. 5(B), the fluorescence values remained above 80 % after the tenth cycle despite the intensity diminished after multiple cycles. Fig. 5(C) shows that the 2SRM-CDs presented visual pH responsiveness, and it appeared different fluorescent colors in acidic and alkaline environments. Some studies have found that the environmental pH level of human cancer cells (<5.2) is typically below the healthy cells (∼7.4), because of the changes in cellular metabolic behavior [45]. The above fact implied that 2SRM-CDs with varied luminescent colors at pH 3–12 might be potentially applied as a good probe to identify the metabolic transformations of human cells.

To access the practicality of present fluorescence nano-sensor, the 2SRM-CDs was applied for quantitative measurement of NFs including FZD, NFT and NFZ under the optimal determination conditions. The fluorescence quenching tests were performed by separately adding different FZD, NFT and NFZ concentrations, and then recorded FL emission spectra. From Fig. 6 (A, B) and C, the FL intensity of 2SRM-CDs solution containing FZD (NFT or NFZ) progressively reduced at the fluorescence emission wavelength of 455 nm with enhancing the amount of FZD (NFT or NFZ). As the amounts of FZD, NFT and NFZ were increased to 330 μM, 260 μM and 230 μM, respectively, the fluorescence intensities of 2SRM-CDs decreased by more than 80 %, and accordingly, the bright blue fluorescence color of 2SRM-CDs disappeared under UV light. As displayed in Fig. 6(D, E) and F, the fluorescence quenching ratios ln (F0/F) and concentrations of FZD within the scopes of 1.0–330 μM (Fig. 6(D)), NFT concentration of 0.5–260 μM (Fig. 6(E)) and NFZ concentration within 0.5–230 μМ (Fig. 5(F)) exhibited ideal linear correlations. The corresponding limits of detection (LODs) were calculated to be 0.354 μM, 0.258 μM and 0.327 μM, respectively (LOD = 3σ/s, σ is standard deviation of blank sample, s represents the linear slope) [46]. In addition, the detection performances of this probe were compared with other reported sensing methods as listed in Table 1 . Obviously, the developed 2SRM-CDs proposed a wider range of linearity or a lower LOD. A good determination performance, low cost, and simplicity of preparation procedures allow it potential for application in measuring NFs.

The selectivity of the developed 2SRM-CDs fluorescent probe towards NFs was assessed by mixing 200 μM of diverse potential interferences such as metal ions, anions, amino acids and antibiotics, respectively. As shown in Fig. 7 (A) , all studied substances except FZD, NFZ, and NFT were unable to significantly reduce the fluorescence intensity of 2SRM-CDs solution, suggesting its good selectivity to NFs. Moreover, the anti-interference tests were further performed by testing the fluorescent intensities of 2SRM-CDs as the NFs were mixed with the interfering substances. From Fig. 7 (B), it was found that the interfering substances had almost no adverse impact on the detection of NFs by 2SRM-CDs probe. Besides, Fig. 7 (C) shows the images of 2SRM-CDs solution with diverse interfering substances as well as NFs under UV light, it was seen that only three NFs could obviously quench the fluorescence of 2SRM-CDs solution. The above results suggested that the newly developed N, S co-doped carbon dots materials were able to serve as a highly selective probe to distinguish NFs from other interfering substances.

The possible quenching mechanism of 2SRM-CDs by NFs was studied by thoroughly researching the properties of 2SRM-CDs, NFs as well as their inter-relationship. First of all, it was observed from Fig. S4(A) that the UV–vis absorption spectra of FZD, NFZ, and NFT visibly overlapped with the fluorescent peaks of excitation and emission of 2SRM-CDs, which suggested that the possible cause of fluorescence quenching was fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) or inner filter effect (IFE) [46,50]. Afterwards, the fluorescence lifetime curves of 2SRM-CDs and 2SRM-CDs + NFs mixture were recorded as presented in Fig. S4(B). It was calculated that the fluorescence lifetimes of 2SRM-CDs after addition of NFZ, NFT and FZD were 5.90 ns, 5.99 ns and 5.68 ns, respectively, which did not significantly reduce the fluorescence lifetime of 2SRM-CDs (6.04 ns). This result not only excluded FRET effect as the main cause of fluorescent weakening, but also revealed that the static quenching instead of dynamic quenching was likely another main cause of fluorescence weakening. According to reported results, the generation of some newly emerging non-fluorescence groups by combination of fluorescent materials with the quencher could be the fundamental reason for static quenching. From Fig. S4(C), (E) and (G), it was seen that the UV–vis absorption peaks of materials showed significant differences after adding NFZ, NFT and FZD, separately, which was evidence of the formation of non-fluorescent groups. To further confirm the static quenching, the Stern-Volmer equation (F0/F = 1 + KSV[Q] = 1 + Kqτ0[Q]) was used to verify the speculate. [7,51]. Based on the linear curves presented in Fig. S4(D), (F) and (H), the KSV values for quenching 2SRM-CDs by NFZ, NFT and FZD had been calculated as 1.6 × 104 L mol−1, 7.0 × 103 L mol−1 and 1.2 × 104 L mol−1, respectively. Correspondingly, the bimolecular quenching constant (Kq) values were 2.7 × 1012 L mol−1s−1, 1.2 × 1012 L mol−1s−1 and 2.0 × 1012 L mol−1s−1, respectively. The three Kq values were significantly greater than the maximum scattering collision quenching constant (2 × 1010 L mol−1 s−1), which further confirmed that the static quenching should be another main cause of fluorescence quenching mechanism [52].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of NFs in actual samples
ce:section.ce:para: For verifying the practicality of the developed probe in detecting NFs including FZD, NFZ, and NFT in real samples, the NFs in lake water and human urine samples were determined by using a frequently-used spiked method. Different concentrations of FZD, NFZ, and NFT (20, 40 and 60 μM) were added into the lake water and human urine samples, and the changes of fluorescence values for 2SRM-CDs were recorded and then the spiked samples were determined on the basis of the linear formulas as presented in Fig. 6. As displayed in Table 2 , the determined results approached the added amounts of NFs very much, and the as-prepared nano-materials had favorable recoveries of NFs from 95.2 to 107.3 %, and good reproducibility and accuracy with RSD below 4 %. These results confirmed the practicality of the developed sensor in detecting NFs in some actual samples.

In this study, an improved method was developed for the preparation of bright blue fluorescent SRM-CDs from biomass derived rapeseed meal. Sulfanilic acid not only provided N, S contents as a dopant, but also effectively broke the chemical structure inside rapeseed cake as acidic catalyst. Aromatic compounds can effectively polymerize into carbon dot materials with the QY of 32.4 % under hydrothermal condition with assistant of sulfanilic acid. The luminescent sites of 2SRM-CDs changed with pH, and therefore exhibited an interesting visual response to pH variation. Besides, NFs can effectively quench the fluorescence of the 2SRM-CDs solution, which proved high sensitivity for furazolidone, nitrofurantoin and nitrofurazone with the LODs of 0.354 μM, 0.258 μM and 0.327 μM, when their concentrations were 1.0–330 μM, 0.5–260 μM and 0.5–230 μM,respectively. The sensor had high selectivity to the interferences such as ions, amino acids and some antibiotics. The quenching reasons were likely attributed to the static quenching and inner filter effect. Furthermore, the as-developed sensor can be successfully used to measure NFs in actual samples such as lake water and human urine, which indicated its potential in fields such as environmental monitoring and food testing.

Ruizhen Guo: Methodology, Investigation. Guoqing Deng: Writing – review & editing, Conceptualization. Hui Wu: Conceptualization. Wenying Fan: Writing – review & editing, Software. Yi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declared that they have no conflicts of interest to this work.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors wanted to acknowledge the financial support by the Hubei Natural Science Foundation Innovation and Development Joint Fund Project (2022CFD099), the Hubei Key Laboratory of Low Dimensional Optoelectronic Material and Devices (HLOM242017).

ce:section.ce:para:
